title: Nutanix CVM Storage Bandwidth Saturation and Uplink Headroom
exclusions: Not valid for a single-node ROBO design where a single NIC pair is both storage and user traffic with no way to read a dedicated CVM storage speed, because the denominator is not meaningful. Excludes dedicated Karbon and NKE worker nodes where the focus is the Kubernetes layer, not the general HCI CVM storage path. Excludes any cluster that only uplinks the one gigabit out-of-band style management path for storage, because the negotiated speed does not match storage intent. Excludes IBM PowerVM estates where a Nutanix CVM is not the storage control point your team expects, because the object model in Splunk and Prism is different and this SPL will not find host_uuid the same way.
evidence: A saved search such as `ss_uc_19_2_29_nutanix_cvm_uplink_saturation_v1` on a fixed schedule, with one CSV or PDF of the final `table` per quarter stored in a restricted `index=evidence` or a CMDB-linked object store, plus a screenshot of Prism cluster network, CVM network, or host NIC details for a sampled `critical` host row, and the `lookups/cvm_uplink_owner.csv` line under change control, satisfies an operational review that this is the bandwidth-axis control, not the latency-only UC-19.2.3 guardrail.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab, ingest synthetic HEC `nutanix:host:throughput` for one host with controller byte-per-second values that, after the Mbps conversion, sit above the governance target for a full four-hour lookback, with `link_speed_in_kbps` set to a known ten or twenty five Gb class number; the search should return `sev0` in warning or high and a `p95_60` above `target_p95_pct` from `cvm_uplink_owner` while `freeze_active` is zero.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A mis-tagged test index where `denom_mbps` is null for every line should yield no results after the `where` that requires `sat_pct`, not a false green row, and a `change_freeze_until` in the future in the lookup should demote a would-be `high` row to `info` for the same synthetic bytes, proving freeze logic without masking `critical`.

dataSources: PRIMARY: Splunk_TA_nutanix (Splunkbase 5076) for Prism REST, including `sourcetype=nutanix:cluster:metrics` and `sourcetype=nutanix:host` (cluster and node inventory, plus host- or cluster-level network and controller context where the TA exposes it on your AOS and TA version). In field references you may also see the underscore-style name `nutanix:cluster_metrics` on some builds; the pipeline treats them as the same class of object. SECONDARY: custom HEC poller to Prism Element `GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/hosts/{host_uuid}/host_nics/` and `.../stats?metrics=hypervisor_avg_io_latency_usecs,storage.usage_bytes,controller.received_bytes_per_second,controller.transmitted_bytes_per_second,controller.read_io_bandwidth_kBps,controller.write_io_bandwidth_kBps&start_time_in_usecs=...&end_time_in_usecs=...&interval_in_secs=30`, and `GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/hosts/{host_uuid}` for CVM and node identity, sourcetypes `nutanix:host:nic`, `nutanix:host:throughput`, and `nutanix:host:identity`. TERTIARY: optional `snmp:if:hc` from Splunk Connect for SNMP on ToR `ifHCInOctets` and `ifHCOutOctets` for CVM-facing ports, to corroborate megabit trends from the switch side. Governance: `cvm_uplink_owner.csv` via transforms.
app: Splunk_TA_nutanix; custom HEC poller for Prism Element REST API stats endpoint; (optional) SC4SNMP for ToR switch corroboration
spl:
`comment("UC-19.2.29 Nutanix CVM storage uplink bandwidth: rolling p95, burst, Mbps vs coalesce link Kbps to Mbps OR expected. SOURCES: nutanix:host:throughput, inputlookup. Context: nutanix:cluster:metrics, nutanix:host. For one minute samples, list window=60 and burst window=3 are about one hour and three minutes; if you re-bin to five minutes first, set list window=12. Tunables: target_p95_pct. earliest=-4h@h. Replace index AND sourcetype if needed.")`
| search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:host:throughput" earliest=-4h@h latest=@h
| eval host_uuid=toString(coalesce(host_uuid, hostUuid, node_uuid, nodeUuid, ""))
| where len(host_uuid)>0
| join type=left max=0 host_uuid [| inputlookup cvm_uplink_owner | eval host_uuid=toString(host_uuid) ]
| eval cr=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_received_bytes_per_second, controllerReceivedBytesPerSecond, "0")), 10)
| eval ct=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_transmitted_bytes_per_second, controllerTransmittedBytesPerSecond, "0")), 10)
| eval lkb=tonumber(toString(coalesce(link_speed_in_kbps, linkSpeedInKbps, "0")), 10)
| eval total_mbps=round(((cr+ct)*8.0)/1000000.0, 4)
| eval cname=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, node_name, nodeName, ""))
| eval sp_mbps=if((lkb>0), lkb/1000.0, null())
| eval elink=tonumber(toString(coalesce(expected_link_speed_mbps, "0")), 10)
| eval denom_mbps=nullif(coalesce(sp_mbps, if((elink>0), elink, null())), 0)
| eval sat_pct=if((isnotnull(denom_mbps) AND (denom_mbps>0) AND (isnotnull(total_mbps))), 100.0*total_mbps/denom_mbps, null())
| where (isnotnull(sat_pct) AND (denom_mbps>0))
| sort 0 host_uuid, _time
| streamstats window=3 current=t global=false sum(eval(if((isnotnull(sat_pct) AND (sat_pct>=90)),1,0))) AS burst3 BY host_uuid
| streamstats window=60 current=t global=false list(sat_pct) AS Lr BY host_uuid
| mvexpand Lr
| eval sat_e=tonumber(Lr, 10)
| stats perc95(sat_e) AS p95_60, max(sat_e) AS peak_12, max(burst3) AS burst3_max, max(_time) AS last_event, first(total_mbps) AS sample_mbps, first(denom_mbps) AS link_or_expect_mbps, first(cname) AS cluster_label, first(owner_team) AS owner_team, first(target_p95_pct) AS target_p95_pct, first(change_freeze_until) AS change_freeze_until BY host_uuid, _time
| eval tgt=tonumber(toString(coalesce(target_p95_pct, "80")), 10)
| eval ft=coalesce(strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"), strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d"))
| eval freeze_active=if((isnotnull(change_freeze_until) AND (len(trim(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, ""))))>0) AND (now() < coalesce(ft, 0))), 1, 0)
| eval sev0=case(( (isnotnull(p95_60) AND (p95_60>=95)) OR ( (isnotnull(burst3_max) AND (burst3_max>=2)) AND (p95_60>=80) )), "critical", ( (isnotnull(p95_60) AND (p95_60>=90)) OR ( (isnotnull(burst3_max) AND (burst3_max>=1)) AND (p95_60>=75) )), "high", ( (isnotnull(p95_60) AND (p95_60>=tgt)) OR (isnotnull(peak_12) AND (peak_12>=95)) ), "warning", 1=1, "info")
| eval sev0=if((freeze_active=1) AND (sev0!="critical"), "info", sev0)
| sort 0 host_uuid, -_time
| dedup 1 host_uuid
| table last_event, host_uuid, cluster_label, p95_60, peak_12, burst3_max, sample_mbps, link_or_expect_mbps, sev0, owner_team, freeze_active, tgt, change_freeze_until
description: Correlates per-node Controller-VM–facing network throughput in megabits per second from the Prism `controller.received_bytes_per_second` and `controller.transmitted_bytes_per_second` pair against negotiated storage-network speed or a governance fallback, then builds a rolling short-window percentile and a burst counter so you can show when guest-VM I/O back-pressure is plausibly caused by the CVM path filling its uplink before you blame disks, processes, or cluster-wide latency. The saved search is intentionally a bandwidth-axis slice you pair with the latency family in UC-19.2.3, not a substitute for the service-state views in UC-19.2.7 and UC-19.2.14 or the resiliency and rebuild work in UC-19.2.9.
value: CVM uplink headroom is the ceiling on how fast storage traffic can leave a node, so when it runs hot, every VM on that node competes for the same few gigabits even if the drives underneath still have IOPS. Making saturation visible by node gives network, platform, and application owners a shared story for uplink upgrades, LACP and switch-side fixes, and targeted VM moves, and it keeps a noisy VM from masquerading as a mystery latency incident when the real limit is the wire.
implementation: Install Splunk_TA_nutanix, deploy the HEC poller and props for `nutanix:host:throughput` with one-minute sample alignment, place `cvm_uplink_owner.csv` with the transforms stanza, paste the `spl` into a report, validate against Prism and optional SC4SNMP, then save as an alert on a fifteen minute schedule with email and webhook, severity routing on the `sev0` field, and throttling on `host_uuid` plus `cluster_label`.
visualization: Line chart: total_mbps per CVM. Heatmap: p95_60 of saturation over twenty-four hours by node. Table: the Step 2 table, sorted on `p95_60`. Optional: overlay panel of UC-19.2.3 latency p95 for the same host id as a two-axis story, not a merged alert.

references.title: Nutanix Developer — Prism REST and automation context
Splunk — Use the HTTP Event Collector

knownFalsePositives: Async or NearSync protection schedules that run wide replication windows to a second site can produce scheduled megabit bursts on the CVM path that are policy-driven, not a surprise fault; tag those windows. Nutanix ADS- or DRS-style storage rebalance, or ingest of a new block after expansion, can legitimately load east-west and storage-facing traffic. LCM or one-click AOS and firmware cycles can briefly change traffic patterns or evacuate guests so the CVM path spikes while health remains green, which is a change window, not a mystery incident. A one-time full resync of a replaced drive or a repair stream after a disk event can hover near the cap for the hours that job needs; you still want this use case to record the fact, but you should classify the ticket with UC-19.2.9 context instead of a pure capacity purchase.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites

This use case is the per-node CVM storage-network bandwidth axis. It is not the shared-storage I/O latency story in UC-19.2.3, not the CVM process availability and Genesis lines in UC-19.2.7, not the per-daemon resource drill in UC-19.2.14, not the AHV vCPU and RAM headroom case in UC-19.2.19, and not the post-fault resync and replication-factor recovery arc in UC-19.2.9. The customer question here is different: the uplink in front of the CVM, measured in megabits per second of read plus write on the controller byte counters, compared to the negotiated wire speed or to a governance fallback, with a rolling short-window saturation percentile and a burst counter. The customer outcome is to separate guest-VM I/O pain that tracks a full storage-network pipe from pain that tracks disk-side latency or CVM process health, and to give network and platform teams a defensible trigger for uplink upgrades, switch work, LACP validation, and noisy-neighbor migration.

You must have the following in place before implementation. (1) Splunk Add-on for Nutanix Prism, folder name Splunk_TA_nutanix, Splunkbase app 5076, on a collection tier with outbound TCP 443 or 9440 to each Prism Element and on search heads for field discoverability. The TA emits cluster and host object streams that matter as primary and secondary context: sourcetype nutanix:cluster:metrics (some documentation still uses the underscore form nutanix:cluster_metrics; treat both as the same family of cluster-level metrics) and sourcetype nutanix:host for inventory, node name, maintenance, and network-related host fields where your build exposes them. Pin the TA in change control; minor field renames are the reason the SPL in Step 2 leans on long coalesce lists. (2) A dedicated wrapper or customer app with local props, transforms, and the HEC stanzas below, without editing Splunk_TA_nutanix default. (3) A CSV lookup file cvm_uplink_owner.csv in lookups with transforms.conf stanza cvm_uplink_owner. Documented columns: host_uuid, cluster_name, owner_team, target_p95_pct, expected_link_speed_mbps, change_freeze_until. The host_uuid value must match the Prism host uuid you pull from the host inventory. expected_link_speed_mbps is the fallback when link_speed_in_kbps is null on a bond or when your poller has not yet merged NIC rows. (4) An HTTP Event Collector token used only for the Prism CVM poller, recommended stanza name hec_prism_cvmnet_v1, secret in your vault. (5) A Prism service account with Cluster Viewer on Prism Element, sufficient to read host stats, host nics, and host identity. (6) Index placement: hci and optional nutanix, plus a netops or snmp index if you add SC4SNMP, with a role that can read them. (7) Polling: one minute for host stats and throughput, thirty minutes for NIC inventory and identity, to balance license, API load, and freshness.

Step 1 — Configure data collection

Network: keep vendor TLS, pin CA files for each Prism, and run the poller on a management host that is not a guest VM on the cluster you stress-test, so a lab outage does not stop telemetry.

The custom poller is mandatory for the byte-per-second pair because those counters live in the host stats namespace that you query with a fixed metrics list, not a generic guess from cluster-only feeds. The exact REST paths you will script are: GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/hosts/{host_uuid}/host_nics/ for link_speed_in_kbps, mac_address, name, host_uuid, is_connected; GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/hosts/{host_uuid}/stats with metrics=hypervisor_avg_io_latency_usecs,storage.usage_bytes,controller.received_bytes_per_second,controller.transmitted_bytes_per_second,controller.read_io_bandwidth_kBps,controller.write_io_bandwidth_kBps and the start_time_in_usecs, end_time_in_usecs, interval_in_secs query parameters; GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/hosts/{host_uuid} for controller_vm_backplane_ip, node_uuid, block_serial, name. The poller should convert the Prism microsecond window fields to epoch seconds for Splunk time assignment, and HEC the merged JSON to sourcetype nutanix:host:throughput. If you prefer separation of concerns, emit nutanix:host:nic and nutanix:host:identity on their own schedules and let a second lightweight transform merge link speed at search time, but the single merged sourcetype is easier for the paste search.

inputs.conf, Heavy Forwarder or Indexer, for the HEC that receives the poller. Replace the token, index, and enable flag with your own values. Do not check in live secrets to git; use a secrets manager in production.

[http://hec_prism_cvmnet_v1]
disabled = 0
indexes = hci
useACK = 1
token = <REDACTED_32_OR_40_HEX>
description = CVM storage-network throughput, NIC, and identity from Prism Element v2.0

Companion script responsibilities: (a) list host_uuid with GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/hosts/ on a five-to-fifteen minute cache, or read the last nutanix:host from Splunk if you already index it; (b) for each host, call the stats URL with a thirty second interval in the example below, a two to five minute time window, and a non-zero interval_in_secs, then parse each returned sample, compute Megabits per second for the CVM data path as (controller.received_bytes_per_second + controller.transmitted_bytes_per_second) times eight divided by 1e6, match or join link_speed_in_kbps from the latest NIC call, and emit one JSON per host per sample with fields host_uuid, cluster_name, controller_received_bytes_per_second, controller_transmitted_bytes_per_second, link_speed_in_kbps, cvm backplane, and a source_time field you round-trip into _time. (c) on HTTP 401, refresh credentials and back off. (d) on empty JSON metrics arrays, log at warn and do not HEC a blank line. (e) thread pool size: cap concurrent hosts to stay under your Prism API rate guidance.

A minimal Python 3.11 sketch using only the standard library is accepted for a lab; production should add structured logging, an OS trust store, and a circuit breaker. The sketch is intentionally short and must be completed with your org auth.

import json, os, ssl, time, urllib.request, urllib.parse

def usecs_to_epoch_s(u):
 return float(u) / 1_000_000.0

def get_json(ctx, url, headers):
 r = urllib.request.Request(url, method="GET")
 for k, v in headers.items():
 r.add_header(k, v)
 with urllib.request.urlopen(r, context=ctx, timeout=60) as resp:
 return json.loads(resp.read().decode("utf-8", "replace"))

def hec_line(hec, token, t_epoch, event_obj, st):
 payload = {
 "time": t_epoch,
 "host": os.environ.get("SPLUNK_HEC_HOST", "prism-collector-01"),
 "source": "prism_cvmnet",
 "sourcetype": st,
 "index": "hci",
 "event": event_obj,
 }
 b = (json.dumps(payload) + "\n").encode("utf-8")
 r = urllib.request.Request(hec, data=b, method="POST")
 r.add_header("Authorization", "Splunk " + token)
 r.add_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
 ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
 with urllib.request.urlopen(r, context=ctx, timeout=30) as resp:
 resp.read(64)

In your app, set props for each sourcetype. Example:

[ nutanix:host:throughput ]
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json

[ nutanix:host:nic ]
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json

[ nutanix:host:identity ]
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json

[ snmp:if:hc ]
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
KV_MODE = none

Sourcetype routing: if a single HEC default sourcetype is not enough, add hec_matcher style routing in a transforms.conf in the same app, or one HEC per sourcetype for clarity. Optional SC4SNMP: map ifHCInOctets and ifHCOutOctets on the ToR to snmp:if:hc, key by switch and ifIndex, and join in a dashboard only; the main alert in Step 2 stays on nutanix:host:throughput to avoid double paging.

The transforms for the owner lookup are minimal:

[ cvm_uplink_owner ]
filename = cvm_uplink_owner.csv
case_sensitive_match = true

On the Splunk_TA_nutanix side, keep modular inputs enabled for nutanix:host so host_uuid, node name, and maintenance are fresh even if your custom poller is offline; that gives you a second path to name resolution for dashboards.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

The canonical search is the one that belongs in the spl field. Schedule it with every-fifteen-minutes in Alert actions, a relative window matching earliest and latest, and a suppress window of at least one hour on host_uuid for warning and at least four hours for high, longer if your operator queue prefers, because a saturated uplink is often a long arc, not a single minute.

When you add alert actions, use two channels: (1) email the owner_team column from the lookup and the platform distribution list, (2) webhook to your incident or chat system with a JSON body that includes host_uuid, cluster_label, p95_60, sev0, and change_freeze_until, so a router can de-duplicate against UC-19.2.3 latency and UC-19.2.9 rebuild. Do not route info with the same on-call as critical; info can go to a daily review report.

A savedsearches.conf fragment for a deployer, not a live secret:

[ nutanix_cvm_uplink_saturation ]
action.email.to = $platform_email_list$
action.webhook.param_url = $webhook_url$
# cron_schedule: use the Splunk schedule UI to pick every fifteen minutes, or paste a valid five field cron for your org
disabled = 0
dispatch.earliest_time = -4h@h
dispatch.latest_time = @h
is_scheduled = 1
alert.severity = 4
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
alert_comparator = greater than
alert_threshold = 0
request.ui_dispatch_app = search

Replace placeholder macros with your own.

The canonical paste-and-run SPL for this use case is:

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.29 Nutanix CVM storage uplink bandwidth: rolling p95, burst, Mbps vs coalesce link Kbps to Mbps OR expected. SOURCES: nutanix:host:throughput, inputlookup. Context: nutanix:cluster:metrics, nutanix:host. For one minute samples, list window=60 and burst window=3 are about one hour and three minutes; if you re-bin to five minutes first, set list window=12. Tunables: target_p95_pct. earliest=-4h@h. Replace index AND sourcetype if needed.")`
| search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:host:throughput" earliest=-4h@h latest=@h
| eval host_uuid=toString(coalesce(host_uuid, hostUuid, node_uuid, nodeUuid, ""))
| where len(host_uuid)>0
| join type=left max=0 host_uuid [| inputlookup cvm_uplink_owner | eval host_uuid=toString(host_uuid) ]
| eval cr=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_received_bytes_per_second, controllerReceivedBytesPerSecond, "0")), 10)
| eval ct=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_transmitted_bytes_per_second, controllerTransmittedBytesPerSecond, "0")), 10)
| eval lkb=tonumber(toString(coalesce(link_speed_in_kbps, linkSpeedInKbps, "0")), 10)
| eval total_mbps=round(((cr+ct)*8.0)/1000000.0, 4)
| eval cname=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, node_name, nodeName, ""))
| eval sp_mbps=if((lkb>0), lkb/1000.0, null())
| eval elink=tonumber(toString(coalesce(expected_link_speed_mbps, "0")), 10)
| eval denom_mbps=nullif(coalesce(sp_mbps, if((elink>0), elink, null())), 0)
| eval sat_pct=if((isnotnull(denom_mbps) AND (denom_mbps>0) AND (isnotnull(total_mbps))), 100.0*total_mbps/denom_mbps, null())
| where (isnotnull(sat_pct) AND (denom_mbps>0))
| sort 0 host_uuid, _time
| streamstats window=3 current=t global=false sum(eval(if((isnotnull(sat_pct) AND (sat_pct>=90)),1,0))) AS burst3 BY host_uuid
| streamstats window=60 current=t global=false list(sat_pct) AS Lr BY host_uuid
| mvexpand Lr
| eval sat_e=tonumber(Lr, 10)
| stats perc95(sat_e) AS p95_60, max(sat_e) AS peak_12, max(burst3) AS burst3_max, max(_time) AS last_event, first(total_mbps) AS sample_mbps, first(denom_mbps) AS link_or_expect_mbps, first(cname) AS cluster_label, first(owner_team) AS owner_team, first(target_p95_pct) AS target_p95_pct, first(change_freeze_until) AS change_freeze_until BY host_uuid, _time
| eval tgt=tonumber(toString(coalesce(target_p95_pct, "80")), 10)
| eval ft=coalesce(strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"), strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d"))
| eval freeze_active=if((isnotnull(change_freeze_until) AND (len(trim(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, ""))))>0) AND (now() < coalesce(ft, 0))), 1, 0)
| eval sev0=case(( (isnotnull(p95_60) AND (p95_60>=95)) OR ( (isnotnull(burst3_max) AND (burst3_max>=2)) AND (p95_60>=80) )), "critical", ( (isnotnull(p95_60) AND (p95_60>=90)) OR ( (isnotnull(burst3_max) AND (burst3_max>=1)) AND (p95_60>=75) )), "high", ( (isnotnull(p95_60) AND (p95_60>=tgt)) OR (isnotnull(peak_12) AND (peak_12>=95)) ), "warning", 1=1, "info")
| eval sev0=if((freeze_active=1) AND (sev0!="critical"), "info", sev0)
| sort 0 host_uuid, -_time
| dedup 1 host_uuid
| table last_event, host_uuid, cluster_label, p95_60, peak_12, burst3_max, sample_mbps, link_or_expect_mbps, sev0, owner_team, freeze_active, tgt, change_freeze_until
```

Key design notes for this SPL: the top-level comment() summarises scope, tunables, owner, and freeze; the join to inputlookup cvm_uplink_owner attaches owner_team and target_p95_pct without breaking on missing rows; the coalesce() pattern protects against camelCase versus snake_case across firmware levels; total_mbps is computed from the byte-per-second pair as ((cr+ct)*8)/1e6; the denominator coalesces the runtime link_speed_in_kbps to expected_link_speed_mbps from the lookup, with nullif() guarding divide-by-zero; streamstats with window=3 counts bursts and window=60 builds the rolling list that feeds perc95(); the case() severity uses both the rolling p95 and the burst counter so a one-minute spike alone cannot trigger high; the freeze_active gate demotes everything except critical to info during a planned change window; and the final | table projects exactly the columns an on-call engineer needs.

Step 3 — Validate

In Prism Element, for the same cluster, open the path Cluster, Network, CVM network, and read the per-node CVM data path. Compare the direction in megabits, not a digit match to four decimals, to the Splunk total_mbps you compute. Then open Hardware, Hosts, the host, NICs, and compare link_speed, bond membership, and whether the CVM backplane and storage VLAN line up with your Splunk fields. This pair is the vendor-side truth for the use case, not a generic IOPS view.

In a change window, on a test VM, run a controlled sequential write with dd to a throwaway disk, sized so you can reach roughly seventy to eighty percent of the expected storage network capacity for a few minutes, then stop. You should see sat_pct and p95_60 move on that host in Splunk, then recover, without a production outage. If you use SC4SNMP, compare the ToR delta in bits per second in the same five minute bucket; slope sign should match.

If Splunk disagrees: compare poll interval, whether Prism averaged over a different chart span, and whether your poller is posting _time to end-of-window or start-of-window; pick one and document it.

Step 4 — Operationalize

Dashboard: row one, line chart, total_mbps for the top N hosts. Row two, heatmap, twenty four hours, color by the rolling p95_60. Row three, the Step 2 table, default sort p95_60 descending, limited to sev0 not equal to info. Row four, optional, a join or side-by-side to UC-19.2.3 latency p95 for the same host, clearly labeled, so a bridge can read bandwidth versus latency.

Runbook: (1) one hot CVM, move a few very chatty guests with Prism, or add Storage QoS class. (2) many hot CVMs on one ToR, expand uplink, fix LAG mis-wire, or split clusters across switches. (3) if UC-19.2.9 shows rebuild and this case shows saturation, use both tickets together, not two unrelated incidents. (4) if UC-19.2.3 shows latency without this case, suspect disk, controller CPU, or guest-side issues first; if this case shows high saturation without large latency, align time range and check whether latency is at a different object scope. (5) add capacity governance: if p95_60 is above your internal standard for a full quarter on the same port, start a buy order, not a fire drill.

Change management: the lookup file, the poller, and the TA are one release; never upgrade Prism without a paired Splunk app release test in lab.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

Case 1, empty stats array from the API. Your time window in micros is too narrow, or the interval_in_secs is zero, or the cluster clock skewed. Widen the window, align the start to a full interval, and re-run one curl in verbose mode, then the poller.

Case 2, null link_speed_in_kbps on a bond while member nics are populated. The denominator must fall back to expected_link_speed_mbps in cvm_uplink_owner, or you must write an eval in the poller that takes the LACP group max. Do not divide by a null in silence.

Case 3, half-duplex or bad negotiation, impossible utilization with low throughput. Cross-check real throughput with switch-side ifHC, then fix speed and duplex.

Case 4, MTU mismatch, retransmits and inflated byte counters without real work completed. Check IP MTU on CVM, hypervisor, and first-hop switch, then retest with ping sweep sizes. Document jumbo and Nutanix storage VLAN in the same runbook as this UC.

Case 5, live migration, storage vMotion, or AHV style moves, large spikes that track job ids in Prism task view. If expected, add a work note, not a sev-1, unless a guest SLO is still violated.

Case 6, post-disk-replacement or repair full-width resync that is expected; pair with UC-19.2.9. If you page on this use case only, the on-call will ignore future real fires, so you must add a work note and cross-link.

Case 7, the join to inputlookup returns no owner. Backfill cvm_uplink_owner for that host or fix host_uuid case mismatch between Prism and the CSV. The search still returns saturation math if bytes are present; governance is thin.

Case 8, duplicate host_uuid in multiple sites with a copied CSV. Add site to the lookup, add site to the HEC event, and filter the base search with site or source cluster to avoid a toxic merge in one table.

Ongoing: once a year, re-read the REST field list in Prism release notes, update coalesce in the poller and the SPL, and re-validate a single block in lab before production. End of document. Vendor regression testing should include a recorded Prism Element upgrade in lab with the same HEC and the same two hosts, and a diff of the JSON keys before you promote the TA. Capacity planning can append one row per month per CVM to a summary index of p95_60, link speed, and expected refresh year so finance sees when uplinks leave the two-year planning band. If you add Prism Central, keep Element as the source of truth for this use case and document any PC to PE proxy delay in the handover note to avoid confusing two time bases. The Splunk time zone on the search head, the poller time zone, and the Prism NTP time zone should all be UTC for operational work, and local time only in email templates. Additional yearly review: confirm SC4SNMP OIDs, confirm firewall rules for 9440, and confirm the service user password rotation.
grandmaExplanation: We check how full the data-carrying link is between the machines that store your virtual disks, not just how long each disk wait feels. If that link is the bottleneck, a lot of users feel it at the same time, and we can tell the people who fix networks and who move virtual machines to spread the load before everyone calls at once.
hardware: Targets Nutanix AOS on AHV and on ESXi-on-Nutanix with ten, twenty five, or one hundred GbE class NICs, including common Mellanox ConnectX-4, ConnectX-5, and ConnectX-6, Intel X710, Intel E810, and Broadcom 57xx families on certified Nutanix NX generations, Dell XC, Lenovo HX, HPE DX, Cisco UCS-C rack nodes, and Inspur or other factory-qualified hyperconverged lines you support. Typical data-center builds use a pair of storage-facing interfaces in a bond: static LAG or LACP to redundant ToR, or an active backup pair to two switches for smaller sites. Hardening should record whether a host negotiates a full twenty five Gb to each leg or a split speed when one path is down, because that changes the true headroom the saturation ratio is measured against.
